desc: Gallipoli works when you want southern Italy to feel properly coastal rather than just pretty: a fortified old town on its own limestone island, warm Ionian water, city beaches you can actually use, and enough Salento energy in summer to make it more vibrant than a quiet heritage stop.
hls: The old town sits on an island linked by a bridge, so Gallipoli feels more dramatic than a standard Apulian beach base.
This is one of the easier places in Puglia to combine historic-core wandering with real swim time on the same day.
The castle, sea walls, baroque churches, and fish-market atmosphere keep the destination from becoming just another beach-club strip.
Spiaggia della Purita gives Gallipoli a rare in-town beach that is woven straight into the historic center.
Summer brings genuine nightlife and domestic-holiday momentum, especially around beach clubs and late evenings.
September is often the sweet spot: the sea stays warm, but the pressure eases compared with peak July-August.

todo.name: Castello di Gallipoli
todo.desc: Start at the waterside fortress by the bridge, because it explains Gallipoli's island-city layout and long defensive history.

todo.name: Centro storico wall walk
todo.desc: Circle the old town slowly for bastions, sea views, and the feeling of the historic core sitting out in the Ionian.

todo.name: Spiaggia della Purita
todo.desc: Use the city beach right under the old town when you want a swim without giving up the historic setting.

todo.name: Sant'Agata Cathedral and Purita church
todo.desc: Make time for Gallipoli's baroque interiors so the town reads as more than walls, water, and nightlife.

todo.name: Harbour seafood evening
todo.desc: Base one dinner around the port and fish-market side of town, where Gallipoli still feels tied to a working maritime identity.

todo.name: Punta Pizzo or Baia Verde side trip
todo.desc: Give one block to the wider Gallipoli coast if you want the softer dune-and-clear-water Salento beach version of the destination.

wifi.note: Usually easy in hotels and modern apartments, though the old town's historic buildings can still mean room-to-room variation and weaker mobile signal in thick-stone properties.

prac.fltNote: Gallipoli has no airport. The normal gateway is Brindisi, which currently has Gatwick nonstops, then a rail, shuttle, or rental-car transfer south into Salento; keep destination-level nonstop flags false because the direct flight is to the gateway, not Gallipoli itself.
prac.lang: Italian; English is common in tourism-facing stays and restaurants, but less automatic away from the seafront and old-town core.
prac.visa: Italy follows Schengen rules, so UK travellers should check passport validity and the usual 90-in-180-day limit before travel.
alerts: July and August bring real crowd, nightlife, and room-price pressure.
The centro storico has seasonal ZTL restrictions, so driving and parking need more planning than first-timers expect.
Beach clubs and popular lidos book up early in peak season, even when the town itself still feels walk-in casual.

sweet: June and especially September are usually Gallipoli's strongest all-round months for warm water, lively atmosphere, and lower pressure than the peak Italian summer rush.
